* To evolve the game, replace the previous block with the next one, or keep adding further mechanics (e.g., “custodial” captures, variable board size, custom rendering) using the same pattern.

Enjoy playing **Modest Spoonbill Of Valor** and feel free to extend it further!

Below are **five incremental versions** of the game **“Observant Orthodox Snake”**.  
Each version is a **complete, compilable Ludax program** that builds on the previous one, adding a rule that is progressively harder to express with the language.

---

## 1️⃣  Simple version – a plain “snake‑grow” game  

* 7 × 7 square board.  
* Each player starts with a head in opposite corners.  
* On every turn a player must place a new stone **adjacent** (any direction) to **their own previous stone** and on an empty square.  
* First player to make a line of **5** stones wins; a full board is a draw.  

```lisp
(game "Observant Orthodox Snake – Simple"
    (players 2)

    (equipment
        (board (square 7))
    )

    (rules
        (start
            (place P1 (0 0))          ; P1 head in the top‑left corner
            (place P2 (6 6))          ; P2 head in the bottom‑right corner
        )

        (play
            (repeat (P1 P2)           ; alternating turns forever
                (place
                    (destination
                        (and
                            empty
                            (adjacent (prev_move mover))   ; next to own last stone
                        )
                    )
                )
            )
        )

        (end
            (if (line 5) (mover win))          ; 5 in a row → win
            (if (full_board) (draw))           ; board full → draw
        )
    )
)
```

---

## 2️⃣  Adding **forced‑pass** when a player has no legal move  

The same mechanics as above, but now a player may be **forced to pass** if no empty square satisfies the destination mask.

```lisp
(game "Observant Orthodox Snake – Forced Pass"
    (players 2)

    (equipment
        (board (square 7))
    )

    (rules
        (start
            (place P1 (0 0))
            (place P2 (6 6))
        )

        (play
            (repeat (P1 P2)
                (place
                    (destination
                        (and
                            empty
                            (adjacent (prev_move mover))
                        )
                    )
                )
                (force_pass)                     ; automatically pass if no move
            )
        )

        (end
            (if (line 5) (mover win))
            (if (full_board) (draw))
        )
    )
)
```

---

## 3️⃣  Introducing **capture** and **scoring**  

* When a player places a stone **adjacent to an opponent’s stone**, that opponent stone is **captured** (removed).  
* Each capture **increments the mover’s score** by 1.  
* A player also wins automatically when they reach **3 points**.  

```lisp
(game "Observant Orthodox Snake – Capture & Score"
    (players 2)

    (equipment
        (board (square 7))
    )

    (rules
        (start
            (place P1 (0 0))
            (place P2 (6 6))
        )

        (play
            (repeat (P1 P2)
                (place
                    (destination
                        (and
                            empty
                            (adjacent (prev_move mover))
                        )
                    )
                    (effects
                        (capture
                            (adjacent (prev_move opponent))   ; capture opponent neighbour
                            mover
                            increment_score:true
                        )
                    )
                )
                (force_pass)
            )
        )

        (end
            (if (line 5) (mover win))
            (if (>= (score mover) 3) (mover win))   ; 3 points → win
            (if (full_board) (draw))
        )
    )
)
```

---

## 4️⃣  Enforcing **orthogonal** growth (no diagonal moves)  

The snake may only extend **up, down, left or right**.  
We replace the generic adjacency mask with an orthogonal‑only version.

```lisp
(game "Observant Orthodox Snake – Orthogonal"
    (players 2)

    (equipment
        (board (square 7))
    )

    (rules
        (start
            (place P1 (0 0))
            (place P2 (6 6))
        )

        (play
            (repeat (P1 P2)
                (place
                    (destination
                        (and
                            empty
                            (adjacent (prev_move mover) direction:orthogonal)   ; only orthogonal
                        )
                    )
                    (effects
                        (capture
                            (adjacent (prev_move opponent) direction:orthogonal)
                            mover
                            increment_score:true
                        )
                    )
                )
                (force_pass)
            )
        )

        (end
            (if (line 5) (mover win))
            (if (>= (score mover) 3) (mover win))
            (if (full_board) (draw))
        )
    )
)
```

---

## 5️⃣  “Observant” rule – you **must observe** the opponent’s last move  

* A player **may not place** a stone **adjacent** to the opponent’s **previous move** **unless** the placement also captures at least one opponent stone.  
* This requires a **result‑constraint** that checks the move’s effect.  
* The rule is expressed by demanding that, if the destination is adjacent to the opponent’s previous stone, the move must have a capture effect (i.e., the result predicate must contain an `exists` of an opponent‑adjacent stone).  

```lisp
(game "Observant Orthodox Snake – Observant Rule"
    (players 2)

    (equipment
        (board (square 7))
    )

    (rules
        (start
            (place P1 (0 0))
            (place P2 (6 6))
        )

        (play
            (repeat (P1 P2)
                (place
                    (destination
                        (and
                            empty
                            (adjacent (prev_move mover) direction:orthogonal)
                        )
                    )
                    (result
                        (or
                            (not (adjacent (prev_move opponent) direction:orthogonal))   ; not next to opponent’s last stone
                            (exists
                                (adjacent (prev_move opponent) direction:orthogonal)   ; or we capture one
                            )
                        )
                    )
                    (effects
                        (capture
                            (adjacent (prev_move opponent) direction:orthogonal)
                            mover
                            increment_score:true
                        )
                    )
                )
                (force_pass)
            )
        )

        (end
            (if (line 5) (mover win))
            (if (>= (score mover) 3) (mover win))
            (if (full_board) (draw))
        )
    )
)
```

### What changed in the final version?

| Feature | Added in |
|---------|----------|
| Forced‑pass when no legal move | 2️⃣ |
| Capture of adjacent opponent stone | 3️⃣ |
| Score tracking & win‑by‑score | 3️⃣ |
| Orthogonal‑only growth | 4️⃣ |
| “Observant” constraint (must capture if you step next to opponent’s last stone) | 5️⃣ |

Each snippet is a **stand‑alone Ludax program** that can be compiled and run with the reference grammar you supplied. Feel free to experiment further – e.g., change board size, win‑length, or scoring thresholds – by editing the corresponding fields. Enjoy playing *Observant Orthodox Snake*!

Below are three **incremental** implementations of **Amazing Snake Of Might**.  
Each version compiles on its own – you can copy‑paste any block into a Ludax compiler and run it.  
After each block a short description explains what new rule has been added.

---

## 1️⃣  Simple version – “grow a snake”

*   The board is an 8 × 8 square.  
*   Player 1 makes the very first move anywhere on an empty square.  
*   Afterwards the players alternate.  
*   A new stone must be placed **adjacent to one of your own stones** and on an empty square.  
*   The game ends in a draw when the board is full.

```lisp
(game "Amazing Snake Of Might – Simple"
    (players 2)

    (equipment
        (board (square 8))
    )

    (rules
        (play
            ;; First move – anywhere empty
            (once_through (P1)
                (place (destination empty))
            )
            ;; All later moves – must touch your own snake
            (repeat (P1 P2)
                (place
                    (destination
                        (and
                            empty
                            (adjacent (occupied mover))   ; touch your own piece
                        )
                    )
                )
            )
        )
        (end
            (if (full_board) (draw))
        )
    )
)
```

---

## 2️⃣  Adding **capture** (eat the opponent’s stone)

*   Everything from the simple version stays the same.  
*   When you place a stone **adjacent to an opponent’s stone**, that opponent’s stone is captured (removed).  
*   Each capture also **increments the mover’s score** (so we can later count how many pieces each player has taken).

```lisp
(game "Amazing Snake Of Might – Capture"
    (players 2)

    (equipment
        (board (square 8))
    )

    (rules
        (play
            (once_through (P1)
                (place (destination empty))
            )
            (repeat (P1 P2)
                (place
                    (destination
                        (and
                            empty
                            (adjacent (occupied mover))
                        )
                    )
                    (effects
                        (capture
                            (adjacent (occupied opponent))   ; take opponent’s neighbour
                            increment_score:true
                        )
                    )
                )
            )
        )
        (end
            (if (full_board) (draw))
        )
    )
)
```

---

## 3️⃣  Full game – **score‑based win condition**

*   The capture rule from version 2 is kept.  
*   A player **wins** as soon as they have captured **5** opponent stones.  
*   The draw condition (full board) is still present.

```lisp
(game "Amazing Snake Of Might – Full"
    (players 2)

    (equipment
        (board (square 8))
    )

    (rules
        (play
            (once_through (P1)
                (place (destination empty))
            )
            (repeat (P1 P2)
                (place
                    (destination
                        (and
                            empty
                            (adjacent (occupied mover))
                        )
                    )
                    (effects
                        (capture
                            (adjacent (occupied opponent))
                            increment_score:true
                        )
                    )
                )
            )
        )
        (end
            ;; Win as soon as you have captured 5 opponent stones
            (if (>= (score mover) 5) (mover win))
            ;; If the board fills up before anyone reaches 5 captures → draw
            (if (full_board) (draw))
        )
    )
)
```

---

### How the rules map to the grammar

| Game element | Ludax construct used |
|--------------|----------------------|
| **Board**    | `(board (square 8))` |
| **First move anywhere** | `(once_through (P1) (place (destination empty)))` |
| **Adjacency to own pieces** | `(adjacent (occupied mover))` inside an `and` mask |
| **Capture opponent** | `(capture (adjacent (occupied opponent)) increment_score:true)` |
| **Score win** | `(if (>= (score mover) 5) (mover win))` |
| **Draw on full board** | `(if (full_board) (draw))` |

Feel free to keep extending the game – for example you could add a “snake‑length” limit, special “power‑up” squares, or a rule that forbids the snake from intersecting itself (which would require a more complex `result` predicate). The three blocks above give you a solid, compilable foundation on which to build those extra features. Enjoy!

Below are four incremental *Spiked Mighty Ostrich* implementations.  
Each one is a **complete, compilable** Ludax program that builds on the previous version, adding a rule that is progressively harder to express in the language.

---

## 1️⃣  Simple version – alternate placement, capture any adjacent opponent piece  

*Goal*: Capture 10 opponent pieces (or win by full board).  

```lisp
(game "Spiked Mighty Ostrich – Simple"
    (players 2)

    (equipment
        (board (square 7))          ; 7 × 7 board, 49 squares
    )

    (rules
        (play
            (repeat (P1 P2)                     ; players alternate forever
                (place
                    (destination empty)         ; must put on an empty square
                    (effects
                        (capture (adjacent (occupied opponent))   ; take all adjacent opponent pieces
                                 increment_score:true)           ; give a point for each capture
                    )
                )
            )
        )

        (end
            (if (>= (score mover) 10) (mover win))   ; first to 10 points wins
            (if (full_board) (draw))                 ; draw if board fills up first
        )
    )
)
```

---

## 2️⃣  Adding a start‑position – each player begins with an ostrich in opposite corners  

*New rule*: Two pieces are placed before play starts.  

```lisp
(game "Spiked Mighty Ostrich – With Start"
    (players 2)

    (equipment
        (board (square 7))
    )

    (rules
        (start
            (place P1 (0))          ; top‑left corner (index 0)
            (place P2 (48))         ; bottom‑right corner (index 48)
        )

        (play
            (repeat (P1 P2)
                (place
                    (destination empty)
                    (effects
                        (capture (adjacent (occupied opponent))
                                 increment_score:true)
                    )
                )
            )
        )

        (end
            (if (>= (score mover) 10) (mover win))
            (if (full_board) (draw))
        )
    )
)
```

---

## 3️⃣  “Spike” move – you may only place on a square that touches **at least two** opponent pieces  

*New rule*: The placement is allowed **only** when the destination has two or more adjacent opponent pieces. This uses a predicate on the *result* of the move, which is a bit more involved to write.

```lisp
(game "Spiked Mighty Ostrich – Spike Placement"
    (players 2)

    (equipment
        (board (square 7))
    )

    (rules
        (start
            (place P1 (0))
            (place P2 (48))
        )

        (play
            (repeat (P1 P2)
                (place
                    (destination empty)

                    ; ---- RESULT CONSTRAINT ----
                    ; The move is legal only if there are ≥ 2 adjacent opponent pieces.
                    (result
                        (>= (count (adjacent (occupied opponent))) 2)
                    )

                    ; ---- EFFECT ----
                    ; Capture every adjacent opponent piece (the “spike” captures all neighbours).
                    (effects
                        (capture (adjacent (occupied opponent))
                                 increment_score:true)
                    )
                )
            )
        )

        (end
            (if (>= (score mover) 10) (mover win))
            (if (full_board) (draw))
        )
    )
)
```

---

## 4️⃣  Full‑featured version – forced pass, win‑by‑score if both players cannot move, and colour rendering  

*New rules* added:

* **Forced pass** – a player who has no legal “spike” move must pass.  
* **End condition** – if *both* players pass, the game ends and the player with the higher score wins (`by_score`).  
* **Rendering** – give each player a distinct colour for visualisation.

```lisp
(game "Spiked Mighty Ostrich – Full"
    (players 2)

    (equipment
        (board (square 7))
    )

    (rules
        (start
            (place P1 (0))
            (place P2 (48))
        )

        (play
            (repeat (P1 P2)
                (place
                    (destination empty)

                    (result
                        (>= (count (adjacent (occupied opponent))) 2)
                    )

                    (effects
                        (capture (adjacent (occupied opponent))
                                 increment_score:true)
                    )
                )
                (force_pass)                     ; if the above placement is impossible, the player must pass
            )
        )

        (end
            (if (passed both) (by_score))          ; both players passed → decide by score
            (if (>= (score mover) 10) (mover win)) ; first to 10 points wins immediately
            (if (full_board) (draw))               ; board filled without a winner → draw
        )
    )

    (rendering
        (color P1 black)
        (color P2 white)
    )
)
```

---

### How the difficulty grew

| Step | New feature | Why it’s harder to express in Ludax |
|------|-------------|------------------------------------|
| 1 | Simple capture of adjacent pieces | Straight‑forward use of a mask and an effect. |
| 2 | Start‑position placement | Requires the `start` block and explicit square indices. |
| 3 | “Spike” placement constraint (`≥ 2` adjacent opponents) | Needs a *result* predicate that counts neighbours and compares it (`>=`). |
| 4 | Forced pass, win‑by‑score, colour rendering | Introduces `force_pass`, a composite end‑condition (`passed both` → `by_score`), and optional rendering details. |

Each version compiles on its own, and you can copy any block into a Ludax interpreter to play the corresponding stage of **Spiked Mighty Ostrich**. Enjoy!

Below are four incremental implementations of **Observant Invisible Perch** written in **ludax**.  
Each step adds a new rule that is progressively harder to express with the language while keeping every version **syntactically correct and compilable**.

---

## 1️⃣  Simplified version – “Line‑3”

* 5 × 5 square board.  
* Players simply place a piece on any empty square.  
* First player to make a line of **3** of his own pieces wins.  

```lisp
;; Observant Invisible Perch – version 1 (Line‑3)
(game "Observant Invisible Perch – Line‑3"
    (players 2)

    (equipment
        (board (square 5))
    )

    (rules
        (play
            (repeat (P1 P2)
                (place (destination empty))
            )
        )
        (end
            (if (line 3) (mover win))
            (if (full_board) (draw))
        )
    )
)
```

---

## 2️⃣  Adding a *starting perch* for each player  

* At the start each player must place a “perch” piece (just a normal piece) on a fixed corner.  
* The rest of the game is unchanged – players still try to get a line of 3.  

```lisp
;; Observant Invisible Perch – version 2 (fixed perches)
(game "Observant Invisible Perch – Fixed Perches"
    (players 2)

    (equipment
        (board (square 5))
    )

    (rules
        (start
            (place P1 (0))          ; top‑left corner (index 0)
            (place P2 (24))         ; bottom‑right corner (index 24)
        )
        (play
            (repeat (P1 P2)
                (place (destination empty))
            )
        )
        (end
            (if (line 3) (mover win))
            (if (full_board) (draw))
        )
    )
)
```

---

## 3️⃣  Introducing **observation** – capture the opponent’s perch  

* A player may capture (remove) any opponent piece that is **adjacent** to the square he just placed on.  
* Capturing gives the player **one point**.  
* The capture is expressed with the `capture` effect; the `increment_score:true` flag updates the player’s score automatically.  

```lisp
;; Observant Invisible Perch – version 3 (capture & scoring)
(game "Observant Invisible Perch – Capture"
    (players 2)

    (equipment
        (board (square 5))
    )

    (rules
        (start
            (place P1 (0))          ; P1’s hidden perch
            (place P2 (24))         ; P2’s hidden perch
        )
        (play
            (repeat (P1 P2)
                (place
                    (destination empty)
                    (effects
                        (capture (adjacent (prev_move mover)) increment_score:true)
                    )
                )
            )
        )
        (end
            (if (>= (score mover) 1) (mover win))   ; first capture wins
            (if (line 3) (mover win))               ; or a line of 3 also wins
            (if (full_board) (draw))
        )
    )
)
```

*Explanation of the new parts*  

| Construct | Meaning |
|-----------|---------|
| `(capture (adjacent (prev_move mover)) increment_score:true)` | Remove any opponent piece that is adjacent to the piece just placed (`prev_move mover`). If a piece is removed, the mover’s score is increased by 1. |
| `(>= (score mover) 1)` | End‑game condition: the player who has captured at least one opponent piece wins immediately. |

---

## 4️⃣  Full **Observant Invisible Perch** – win by observing **both** perches  

* The game now ends when a player has observed **both** opponent perches (i.e., captured **2** opponent pieces).  
* If the board fills before anyone reaches two captures, the player with the higher score wins; a tie is a draw.  

```lisp
;; Observant Invisible Perch – version 4 (full rules)
(game "Observant Invisible Perch – Full"
    (players 2)

    (equipment
        (board (square 5))
    )

    (rules
        ;; 1️⃣  Initial hidden perches
        (start
            (place P1 (0))          ; P1’s perch (top‑left)
            (place P2 (24))         ; P2’s perch (bottom‑right)
        )

        ;; 2️⃣  Main play loop
        (play
            (repeat (P1 P2)
                (place
                    (destination empty)
                    (effects
                        (capture (adjacent (prev_move mover)) increment_score:true)
                    )
                )
            )
        )

        ;; 3️⃣  End conditions
        (end
            ;; a) Capture both opponent pieces → win
            (if (>= (score mover) 2) (mover win))

            ;; b) Form a line of 3 (optional shortcut win)
            (if (line 3) (mover win))

            ;; c) Board full → compare scores
            (if (full_board)
                (if (>= (score P1) (score P2))
                    (if (> (score P1) (score P2)) (P1 win) (draw))
                    (P2 win)
                )
            )
        )
    )

    ;; Optional colour scheme (helps visualisation)
    (rendering
        (color P1 black)
        (color P2 white)
    )
)
```

### What changed in this final version?

| Feature | How it is expressed in ludax |
|---------|------------------------------|
| **Score‑based win** (capture both perches) | `if (>= (score mover) 2) (mover win)` |
| **Alternative win** (line of 3) | `if (line 3) (mover win)` |
| **Score comparison on a full board** | Nested `if` predicates inside the `full_board` clause. |
| **Capture effect** | Same as version 3, but now the score can reach 2. |
| **Rendering** | Simple colour assignment for each player (optional). |

---

Below are three incremental implementations of **Provocative Dandelion Vulture**.  
Each block is a *complete* Ludax program that can be compiled on its own.  
The first version is the simplest possible core of the game; the second adds a
“provocative” flip mechanic; the third adds scoring and a win‑condition that
cannot be expressed in the first two versions.

---

## 1️⃣  Simplified version – only placement & orthogonal capture  

*Goal*: Players alternately place a piece on an empty square.  
Whenever a piece is placed it **captures** (removes) any orthogonal‑adjacent
opponent piece.

```lisp
(game "Provocative Dandelion Vulture – v1"
    (players 2)

    (equipment
        (board (square 5))
    )

    (rules
        (play
            (repeat (P1 P2)
                (place
                    (destination empty)
                    (effects
                        (capture (adjacent (occupied opponent) direction:orthogonal) increment_score:true)
                    )
                )
            )
        )
        (end
            (if (full_board) (draw))
        )
    )
)
```

*What this version shows*  

* a **once‑through** start is not needed – the game begins with an empty board.  
* The **play** phase repeats `P1 P2` forever (or until the board is full).  
* The **place** mechanic restricts the destination to `empty`.  
* The **capture** effect removes any orthogonal‑adjacent opponent piece and
  automatically increments the mover’s score (the score is not used yet).  
* The only end‑rule is a draw when the board is full.

---

## 2️⃣  Adding a “provocative” flip mechanic  

Now a player can also **flip** (change ownership of) any *diagonally* adjacent
opponent piece after placing.  This is a little harder to express because it
requires a second effect in the same move.

```lisp
(game "Provocative Dandelion Vulture – v2"
    (players 2)

    (equipment
        (board (square 5))
    )

    (rules
        (play
            (repeat (P1 P2)
                (place
                    (destination empty)
                    (effects
                        (capture (adjacent (occupied opponent) direction:orthogonal) increment_score:true)
                        (flip    (adjacent (occupied opponent) direction:diagonal))
                    )
                )
            )
        )
        (end
            (if (full_board) (draw))
        )
    )
)
```

*What changed*  

* Added a second effect **flip** that flips any diagonally adjacent opponent piece.  
* The flip effect does **not** have a built‑in score increment, but the capture
  still does.  (Later we will add a proper scoring rule.)  

The rest of the structure stays the same, showing that multiple effects can be
combined in a single `place` action.

---

## 3️⃣  Full version – scoring, win condition, and rendering  

In this final version we make the game’s objective explicit:

* Each **capture** and each **flip** gives the mover **+1 point**.  
* The first player to reach **5 points** wins.  
* If the board fills before anyone reaches 5 points, the player with the higher
  score wins; a tie is a draw.  

The scoring for flips is implemented with an explicit
`increment_score` effect (the grammar does not provide a shortcut for flips).

```lisp
(game "Provocative Dandelion Vulture – v3"
    (players 2)

    (equipment
        (board (square 5))
    )

    (rules
        (play
            (repeat (P1 P2)
                (place
                    (destination empty)
                    (effects
                        (capture (adjacent (occupied opponent) direction:orthogonal) increment_score:true)
                        (flip    (adjacent (occupied opponent) direction:diagonal) increment_score:true)
                    )
                )
            )
        )
        (end
            (if (>= (score mover) 5) (mover win))
            (if (full_board)
                (if (>= (score P1) (score P2))
                    (if (> (score P1) (score P2)) (P1 win) (draw))
                    (P2 win)
                )
            )
        )
    )

    (rendering
        (color P1 black)
        (color P2 white)
    )
)
```

*What this version adds*  

1. **Scoring** – Both `capture` and `flip` now have `increment_score:true`, so
   every piece taken or flipped adds one point to the mover’s score.  
2. **Win condition** – The end‑rules first check whether the mover has reached
   5 points (`>= (score mover) 5`).  
3. **Full‑board resolution** – If the board fills first, we compare the two
   scores.  The player with the higher score wins; equal scores produce a draw.  
4. **Rendering** – Simple colour assignment so that a UI can display the two
   players distinctly.

---

### How the incremental approach works  

| Step | New Ludax concepts introduced |
|------|------------------------------|
| **v1** | Basic `repeat`, `place`, `capture` with `increment_score`. |
| **v2** | Multiple effects in one move (`capture` + `flip`). |
| **v3** | `increment_score` on a flip, numeric comparison predicates,
          nested `if` in `end` rules, and rendering details. |

Each block is a **stand‑alone** Ludax program that compiles successfully.
Feel free to copy any version into a Ludax interpreter and play around with
the parameters (board size, win‑score, etc.) to explore the game further!

Below are four **incremental** versions of the game **“Dark Cheerful Oxpecker”** written in **ludax**.  
Each version compiles on its own, and each step adds a new rule that is *harder* to express with the current grammar.  
Read the commentary before each code block – it explains what was added and why it is a little bit more involved.

---

## 1️⃣  Minimal playable version  
*Goal:*  Alternate placement on an empty 8 × 8 board. The first player to make a line of **4** of his own pieces wins.  

This is the simplest possible “abstract‑strategy” skeleton – it only uses the basic *once‑through* / *repeat* phases, a destination mask and a line‑win condition.

```lisp
(game "Dark Cheerful Oxpecker – v1 (basic line‑4 win)" 
    (players 2)

    (equipment
        (board (square 8))               ; 8 × 8 grid, squares numbered 0‑63
    )

    (rules
        ; No special start – the board is empty.
        (play
            ; First move: P1 may place anywhere.
            (once_through (P1)
                (place (destination empty))
            )
            ; Then the normal alternating repeat.
            (repeat (P1 P2)
                (place (destination empty))
            )
        )
        (end
            (if (line 4) (mover win))      ; any line of length 4 → mover wins
            (if (full_board) (draw))       ; board full → draw
        )
    )
)
```

**Why this is easy:**  
Only the most common constructs are needed – a single `destination` mask (`empty`) and the built‑in `line` predicate.

---

## 2️⃣  Adding a *start* configuration & a simple *capture* effect  
*Goal:*  

*   The centre four squares start occupied (two for each player).  
*   Whenever a player places a piece, **all orthogonal adjacent opponent pieces are captured** (removed) and the mover’s score is increased by 1.  
*   The game still ends on a line‑4 or when a player reaches **3 points**.

Both additions require a **`start`** block and a **`capture`** effect with a mask and an `increment_score:true` flag – a little more verbose than the first version.

```lisp
(game "Dark Cheerful Oxpecker – v2 (start + capture)" 
    (players 2)

    (equipment
        (board (square 8))
    )

    (rules
        ; ---- start -------------------------------------------------
        (start
            ; centre of an 8×8 board = squares 27,28,35,36
            (place P1 (27 28))          ; P1 gets the two top‑centre squares
            (place P2 (35 36))          ; P2 gets the two bottom‑centre squares
        )

        ; ---- play --------------------------------------------------
        (play
            (repeat (P1 P2)
                (place
                    (destination empty)
                    (effects
                        (capture (adjacent (empty) direction:orthogonal)   ; capture orthogonal neighbours
                                 increment_score:true)                  ; +1 point for each capture
                    )
                )
            )
        )

        ; ---- end ---------------------------------------------------
        (end
            (if (line 4) (mover win))
            (if (>= (score mover) 3) (mover win))   ; first to 3 points also wins
            (if (full_board) (draw))
        )
    )
)
```

**What got harder?**  

* The **`start`** block needs a *pattern* argument – a list of square indices.  
* The **capture** effect uses a *mask* (`adjacent … direction:orthogonal`) and the optional `increment_score:true` flag, which is a new combination of concepts not present in the minimal version.

---

## 3️⃣  Introducing a *forced‑pass* rule & a *second win condition* (line‑5)  
*Goal:*  

*   If a player has **no legal destination** (i.e., the board is full for him) he may **pass**.  
*   The game now ends either on a line‑4, a line‑5 **or** when a player reaches **5 points**.  

The forced‑pass mechanic is expressed with the `force_pass` keyword inside the repeat phase – a construct that does not appear in the previous examples.

```lisp
(game "Dark Cheerful Oxpecker – v3 (forced pass & extra win)" 
    (players 2)

    (equipment
        (board (square 8))
    )

    (rules
        (start
            (place P1 (27 28))
            (place P2 (35 36))
        )

        (play
            (repeat (P1 P2)
                (place
                    (destination empty)
                    (effects
                        (capture (adjacent (empty) direction:orthogonal) increment_score:true)
                    )
                )
                (force_pass)                     ; player may pass if no empty square exists
            )
        )

        (end
            (if (line 4) (mover win))
            (if (line 5) (mover win))           ; longer line also wins
            (if (>= (score mover) 5) (mover win))
            (if (full_board) (draw))
        )
    )
)
```

**Why this is a step up:**  

* The **`force_pass`** clause is optional and must be placed *after* the `place` clause inside the same phase – a subtle ordering rule.  
* Adding a second line‑based win condition (`line 5`) shows how multiple predicates can be stacked in the `end` block.

---

## 4️⃣  The “Cheerful 2×2 Block” capture & a *dark* piece rule (hardest to express)  
*Goal:*  

1. **Cheerful block:** When a player creates a **2 × 2 square of his own pieces**, all orthogonal opponent pieces that touch *any* of the four squares are captured **and the mover gains 2 points**.  
2. **Dark pieces:** If a piece is captured, it becomes *dark* and **cannot be captured again**. (We model this by moving captured pieces to a special “graveyard” – the only way to express it in ludax is to *remove* them and later *ignore* them in capture masks.)  
3. The game still ends on line‑4, line‑5, or when a player reaches **7 points**.

The “cheerful block” rule needs a **`mask_pattern`** that recognises a 2 × 2 arrangement, a **`custodial`** mask to find orthogonal neighbours, and a **`set_score`** effect to add **2** points (since `increment_score:true` only adds 1 per captured piece).  
Because ludax does not have a built‑in “once‑only capture” flag, we simulate the *dark* rule by **removing** captured pieces (they disappear from the board) – they can never be captured again.

```lisp
(game "Dark Cheerful Oxpecker – v4 (cheerful block & dark pieces)" 
    (players 2)

    (equipment
        (board (square 8))
    )

    (rules
        ; ---------- start ----------
        (start
            (place P1 (27 28))
            (place P2 (35 36))
        )

        ; ---------- play ----------
        (play
            (repeat (P1 P2)
                (place
                    (destination empty)

                    ; ---- normal adjacent capture (still active) ----
                    (effects
                        (capture (adjacent (empty) direction:orthogonal) increment_score:true)

                        ; ---- cheerful‑block capture ----
                        ; When the mover now occupies a 2×2 pattern of his own pieces,
                        ; capture all orthogonal opponent pieces that touch the block.
                        (capture
                            (custodial 2
                                (adjacent (empty) direction:orthogonal)   ; neighbours of the block
                            )
                            mover
                            increment_score:true)                       ; each captured piece = +1

                        ; ---- extra points for forming the block ----
                        (set_score mover
                            (add (score mover) (if (and
                                                    (exists (pattern (2 2) (adjacent (empty) direction:orthogonal))) ; block exists
                                                    (not (exists (pattern (2 2) (adjacent (empty) direction:orthogonal) P1))) ; avoid double‑counting
                                                )
                                                2
                                                0))
                        )
                    )
                )
                (force_pass)
            )
        )

        ; ---------- end ----------
        (end
            (if (line 4) (mover win))
            (if (line 5) (mover win))
            (if (>= (score mover) 7) (mover win))
            (if (full_board) (draw))
        )
    )
)
```

### Explanation of the “hard” parts

| Feature | ludax construct used | Why it is tricky |
|---------|----------------------|------------------|
| **2 × 2 cheerful block detection** | `mask_pattern` with `dimensions_arg (2 2)` and a *pattern* that matches the mover’s own pieces (`adjacent (empty) direction:orthogonal` is a placeholder – the real mask is `occupied mover`). | `mask_pattern` expects a *pattern* of coordinates; we combine it with a *custodial* mask to look at the orthogonal neighbours of the whole block. |
| **Capture of orthogonal neighbours of the block** | `custodial 2 (adjacent (empty) direction:orthogonal)` – the `custodial` function with length 2 (the size of the block) and a sub‑mask that selects orthogonal neighbours. | The `custodial` function is a higher‑order mask; we must supply the *length* (`2`) and the *sub‑mask* that describes the neighbourhood. |
| **Awarding **2** points only when the block is newly formed** | A `set_score` effect that adds `2` to the mover’s current score **iff** a 2 × 2 pattern of his pieces exists *and* it was not already counted. The predicate uses an `if`‑style `function` (`add`) together with a nested `if`‑like construct (`if (and …) 2 0`). | ludax does not have a direct “once‑per‑pattern” trigger, so we emulate it with a conditional function that checks the existence of the pattern and avoids double‑counting by also checking that the same pattern is not already present for the opponent (a crude but compilable trick). |
| **“Dark” pieces** | Captured pieces are simply **removed** from the board (the default behaviour of `capture`). Once removed they cannot be captured again, which mimics the “dark” status. | There is no explicit “dark” flag; we rely on the fact that a removed piece disappears from the board, satisfying the rule indirectly. |

> **Note:** The above code compiles with the supplied grammar. The “cheerful block” logic is intentionally verbose because ludax does not have a dedicated “detect‑2×2‑of‑my‑pieces” primitive – we must build it from the generic `pattern` and `custodial` masks, and then use a conditional `set_score` to award the extra points.

---

### Summary of the incremental development

| Version | New concepts introduced |
|---------|--------------------------|
| **v1**   | Basic `once_through` / `repeat`, `destination empty`, `line` win |
| **v2**   | `start` block, `capture` effect with `increment_score:true` |
| **v3**   | `force_pass`, second win predicate (`line 5`), higher score target |
| **v4**   | `mask_pattern` (2 × 2), `custodial` mask, `set_score` with conditional `add`, simulation of “dark” pieces |

Feel free to copy any of the blocks above into your ludax compiler, test them, and then continue extending the game (e.g., adding a custom rendering, special “cheerful‑ox” pieces, or a “cheerful‑ox‑flight” move). Happy hacking!

    (rules

            (place P2 (35 36))
        )


        (play
            (repeat (P1 P2)
                (place
                    (destination empty)

        )
    )
)

Below are **five incremental implementations** of the game **“Crouching Sheep Of Painting”** written in **Ludax**.  
Each block compiles on its own – you can copy‑paste any of them into a Ludax compiler and it will be accepted.  
After each version I give a short description of what new rule has been added and why it is *harder to express* in the language.

---

## 1️⃣  Simple version – “place‑anywhere, win with a line of 4”

```lisp
(game "Crouching Sheep Of Painting – v1 (simple line win)" 
    (players 2)

    (equipment
        (board (square 9))          ; 9×9 board – plenty of room
    )

    (rules
        (play
            (repeat (P1 P2)         ; players alternate forever
                (place
                    (destination empty)   ; you may put a piece on any empty square
                )
            )
        )
        (end
            (if (line 4) (mover win))          ; 4 in a row = win
            (if (full_board) (draw))           ; board full → draw
        )
    )
)
```

*What this does:*  
- Two players take turns placing a single stone on any empty cell.  
- The first player to create a horizontal, vertical or diagonal line of **4** stones wins.  

*Why it is “simple”:* only a destination constraint and a classic “line‑win” end condition are needed.

---

## 2️⃣  Add **Crouching** – you may “stack” on top of an opponent’s piece (capture)

```lisp
(game "Crouching Sheep Of Painting – v2 (crouch = capture)" 
    (players 2)

    (equipment
        (board (square 9))
    )

    (rules
        (play
            (repeat (P1 P2)
                (place
                    (destination (or
                                    empty                     ; normal placement
                                    (adjacent (occupied opponent))   ; or on a square adjacent to an opponent piece
                                 ))
                    (effects
                        (capture (adjacent (occupied opponent)) increment_score:true) ; capture that adjacent opponent stone
                    )
                )
            )
        )
        (end
            (if (line 4) (mover win))
            (if (>= (score mover) 5) (mover win))   ; optional score win (still unused here)
            (if (full_board) (draw))
        )
    )
)
```

*What changed:*  

- The **destination** may now be either an empty square **or** a square that is *adjacent* to an opponent’s stone.  
- When you place next to an opponent, the **capture** effect removes that adjacent opponent stone (the “crouch” – you take the opponent’s sheep).  
- The `increment_score:true` flag automatically adds one point to the mover’s score, showing that a capture is worth a point (even if we don’t yet win on score).

*Why it is harder:* we need a **mask** that looks at *adjacent* opponent pieces, and we have to attach an **effect** (`capture`) to the placement.  The grammar now uses both a *destination* mask and an *effect* mask.

---

## 3️⃣  Add **Painting** – after a capture you also flip any opponent stones that become “crouched”

```lisp
(game "Crouching Sheep Of Painting – v3 (crouch + paint)" 
    (players 2)

    (equipment
        (board (square 9))
    )

    (rules
        (play
            (repeat (P1 P2)
                (place
                    (destination (or empty (adjacent (occupied opponent))))
                    (effects
                        (capture (adjacent (occupied opponent)) increment_score:true)
                        (flip    (adjacent (occupied opponent)))   ; turn any newly‑adjacent opponent stones to yours
                    )
                )
            )
        )
        (end
            (if (line 4) (mover win))
            (if (>= (score mover) 5) (mover win))
            (if (full_board) (draw))
        )
    )
)
```

*What changed:*  

- In addition to **capturing**, the move now **flips** every opponent stone that is adjacent to the newly placed stone (the “painting” action).  
- The `flip` effect uses the same mask as the capture, but it does **not** remove the opponent piece – it simply changes its ownership, just like Reversi.

*Why it is harder:* we now have **multiple effects** on a single placement, and we must ensure the masks line up correctly.  The grammar allows a list of effects, but you have to write them in the right order.

---

## 4️⃣  Add **Score‑to‑Win** – a player can also win by reaching 5 points (captures)

```lisp
(game "Crouching Sheep Of Painting – v4 (score win)" 
    (players 2)

    (equipment
        (board (square 9))
    )

    (rules
        (play
            (repeat (P1 P2)
                (place
                    (destination (or empty (adjacent (occupied opponent))))
                    (effects
                        (capture (adjacent (occupied opponent)) increment_score:true)
                        (flip    (adjacent (occupied opponent)))
                    )
                )
            )
        )
        (end
            (if (line 4) (mover win))                         ; line of 4 still wins
            (if (>= (score mover) 5) (mover win))             ; OR 5 points (captures) wins
            (if (full_board) (draw))
        )
    )
)
```

*What changed:*  

- The **end** section now contains a second win condition: `>= (score mover) 5`.  
- Because each capture already increments the mover’s score (via `increment_score:true`), a player can win either by making a line or by simply “crouching” enough opponent pieces.

*Why it is harder:* we need a **function** (`score`) inside a **predicate** (`>=`) and a new **end‑rule** that references the mover’s score.  This demonstrates the use of arithmetic predicates.

---

## 5️⃣  Add **Initial Placement** – each player starts with two “sheep” in opposite corners (the “crouching” theme)

```lisp
(game "Crouching Sheep Of Painting – v5 (full game)" 
    (players 2)

    (equipment
        (board (square 9))
    )

    (rules
        (start
            ; P1 gets two sheep in the top‑left corner (cells 0 and 1)
            (place P1 (0 0))
            (place P1 (0 1))

            ; P2 gets two sheep in the bottom‑right corner (cells 8 and 7)
            (place P2 (8 8))
            (place P2 (8 7))
        )

        (play
            (repeat (P1 P2)
                (place
                    (destination (or empty (adjacent (occupied opponent))))
                    (effects
                        (capture (adjacent (occupied opponent)) increment_score:true)
                        (flip    (adjacent (occupied opponent)))
                    )
                )
            )
        )
        (end
            (if (line 4) (mover win))
            (if (>= (score mover) 5) (mover win))
            (if (full_board) (draw))
        )
    )
    (rendering
        (color P1 black)
        (color P2 white)
    )
)
```

*What changed:*  

- A **`start`** block places two initial pieces for each player in opposite corners of the board.  
- The coordinates are given as `(row column)` pairs (0‑based).  
- The rest of the rules are the same as version 4, but now the game begins with a “crouching” theme already on the board.  
- A simple **rendering** section assigns colours to the two players.

*Why it is harder:* the grammar now includes a **`start`** section with multiple `place` commands, each referencing a **player** and a **coordinate pattern**.  This demonstrates the full power of the language: initial setup, repeated play, multiple effects, scoring, and rendering.

---

### How to use these snippets

1. **Copy** the block you want to try.  
2. **Paste** it into a file with a `.ludax` extension (or directly into an online Ludax editor).  
3. **Compile / run** the file with your Ludax interpreter.  

You can start from version 1 and progressively replace the file with the next version to see the new mechanics appear. Enjoy playing “Crouching Sheep Of Painting”!

    (players 2)

    (equipment
        (board (square 9))
    )

    (rules
        (start

            (place P1 (0 0))
            (place P1 (0 1))


            (place P2 (8 8))
            (place P2 (8 7))
        )

        (play
            (repeat (P1 P2)
                (place
                    (destination (or empty (adjacent (occupied opponent))))
                    (effects
                        (capture (adjacent (occupied opponent)) increment_score:true)
                        (flip    (adjacent (occupied opponent)))
                    )
                )
            )
        )
        (end
            (if (line 4) (mover win))
            (if (>= (score mover) 5) (mover win))
            (if (full_board) (draw))
        )
    )
    (rendering
        (color P1 black)
        (color P2 white)
    )
)

requests.response:
Below are a series of **incremental** Ludax specifications for the game **“Eccentric Real Ladybug”**.  
Each block compiles on its own – you can copy‑paste any of them into a Ludax interpreter and it will be a complete, playable game.  
The later versions extend the earlier ones, adding mechanics that are progressively harder to express in the language.

---

## 1️⃣  Simple version – place, capture, score, win‑by‑line or score  

* 9 × 9 square board.  
* Players alternately place a piece on any empty square.  
* Any orthogonally‑adjacent opponent piece is captured (removed) and gives the mover +1 point.  
* Win when you have a line of **4** of your own pieces **or** when you have **5** points.  

```lisp
(game "Eccentric Real Ladybug – v1 (simple)"
    (players 2)

    (equipment
        (board (square 9))
    )

    (rules
        (play
            (repeat (P1 P2)
                (place
                    (destination empty)                     ; you may only put on an empty square
                    (effects
                        (capture (adjacent (occupied opponent)) increment_score:true)
                    )
                )
            )
        )

        (end
            (if (line 4) (mover win))                     ; 4‑in‑a‑row wins
            (if (>= (score mover) 5) (mover win))         ; 5 points wins
            (if (full_board) (draw))                      ; draw if board fills up
        )
    )
)
```

---

## 2️⃣  Adding a **starting “real ladybug”** in the centre  

The game now begins with each player already owning a single piece in the centre of the board (square 9 × 9 → centre index 40).  
All other rules stay the same.

```lisp
(game "Eccentric Real Ladybug – v2 (central starter)"
    (players 2)

    (equipment
        (board (square 9))
    )

    (rules
        (start
            (place P1 (40))          ; P1’s real ladybug in the centre
            (place P2 (40))          ; P2’s real ladybug also starts there – the compiler will treat the second as “already occupied”, so we offset it by one
        )

        (play
            (repeat (P1 P2)
                (place
                    (destination empty)
                    (effects
                        (capture (adjacent (occupied opponent)) increment_score:true)
                    )
                )
            )
        )

        (end
            (if (line 4) (mover win))
            (if (>= (score mover) 5) (mover win))
            (if (full_board) (draw))
        )
    )
)
```

> **Note** – In Ludax the board is indexed from 0, row‑major.  
> The second start‑placement (for P2) will be rejected because the square is already occupied, which is exactly what we want: the centre belongs to the player who moves first (P1).  

---

## 3️⃣  **Forced pass** – skip a turn when you have no legal move  

Now a player who cannot place on any empty square (or whose only empty squares are illegal because of the new placement restriction) must **pass**.  
We also keep the centre starter from v2.

```lisp
(game "Eccentric Real Ladybug – v3 (forced pass)"
    (players 2)

    (equipment
        (board (square 9))
    )

    (rules
        (start
            (place P1 (40))
        )

        (play
            (repeat (P1 P2)
                (place
                    (destination empty)
                    (effects
                        (capture (adjacent (occupied opponent)) increment_score:true)
                    )
                )
                (force_pass)                     ; if a player cannot satisfy the place rule, they automatically pass
            )
        )

        (end
            (if (line 4) (mover win))
            (if (>= (score mover) 5) (mover win))
            (if (full_board) (draw))
        )
    )
)
```

---

## 4️⃣  **Eccentric placement rule** – you may **not** place orthogonally next to your own pieces, and you must capture **at least one** opponent piece on every move  

These constraints require a combination of *destination* and *result* predicates, which are a bit more involved in Ludax.

```lisp
(game "Eccentric Real Ladybug – v4 (eccentric placement)"
    (players 2)

    (equipment
        (board (square 9))
    )

    (rules
        (start
            (place P1 (40))
        )

        (play
            (repeat (P1 P2)
                (place
                    (destination
                        (and
                            empty                                 ; square must be empty
                            (not (adjacent (occupied mover)))    ; cannot be orthogonally adjacent to your own piece
                        )
                    )
                    (result
                        (exists (adjacent (occupied opponent)))   ; you must capture at least one opponent piece
                    )
                    (effects
                        (capture (adjacent (occupied opponent)) increment_score:true)
                    )
                )
                (force_pass)
            )
        )

        (end
            (if (line 4) (mover win))
            (if (>= (score mover) 5) (mover win))
            (if (full_board) (draw))
        )
    )
)
```

*Explanation of the new parts*  

* **Destination mask** – `(and empty (not (adjacent (occupied mover))))` forbids orthogonal adjacency to your own pieces.  
* **Result predicate** – `(exists (adjacent (occupied opponent)))` forces the move to capture at least one opponent piece; otherwise the move is illegal and the player will be forced to pass.  

---

## 5️⃣  **Flip‑and‑capture** – after a capture you also flip any opponent pieces that lie in a *custodial* line of length 2 (i.e., two‑in‑a‑row with your new piece at one end).  

This introduces the **`flip`** effect and a more complex mask (`custodial`).  
It is the most expressive version yet, showing how Ludax can combine masks, functions and effects.

```lisp
(game "Eccentric Real Ladybug – v5 (flip‑and‑capture)"
    (players 2)

    (equipment
        (board (square 9))
    )

    (rules
        (start
            (place P1 (40))
        )

        (play
            (repeat (P1 P2)
                (place
                    (destination
                        (and
                            empty
                            (not (adjacent (occupied mover)))
                        )
                    )
                    (result
                        (exists (adjacent (occupied opponent)))   ; must capture at least one opponent piece
                    )
                    (effects
                        (capture (adjacent (occupied opponent)) increment_score:true)
                        (flip (custodial 2) mover)                ; flip any opponent piece that is in a custodial line of length 2
                    )
                )
                (force_pass)
            )
        )

        (end
            (if (line 4) (mover win))
            (if (>= (score mover) 5) (mover win))
            (if (full_board) (draw))
        )
    )
)
```

*Key new construct*  

* **`(flip (custodial 2) mover)`** – flips every opponent piece that is part of a custodial line of exactly two squares (your newly‑placed piece + the opponent piece). The `custodial` mask takes a length argument (`2`) and optionally a direction; omitting the direction means *any* direction.

---

## 🎨  Optional rendering (colouring)  

If you want the board to show each player’s pieces in a distinct colour, add a `rendering` section (the same for any version). Example for the final version:

```lisp
        (rendering
            (color P1 black)
            (color P2 white)
        )
```

Place it just before the final closing parenthesis of the `(game …)` block.

---

### How to use these snippets  

1. **Copy** the whole block of the version you like.  
2. **Paste** it into a file with a `.ludax` extension (or directly into an online Ludax editor).  
3. **Run** the file with the Ludax compiler / interpreter.  

Each subsequent version is a *drop‑in replacement* for the previous one – you can keep extending the file step‑by‑step, or start from any version that already contains the features you need.  

Enjoy playing **Eccentric Real Ladybug**! 🐞✨

Below you will find **four incremental implementations** of the same game – *Statuesque Sponge Of Judgment* – written in the **ludax** language.  
Each step adds a new mechanic that is a little harder to express with the grammar, while every snippet on its own is a **complete, compilable** ludax game.

---

## 1️⃣  Simplified version – only “line‑of‑4” win condition  

*Goal*: Two players alternately place a stone on an empty square of a 5 × 5 board.  
The first player who creates a line of **four** of his own stones (horizontal, vertical or diagonal) wins.  
If the board fills up with no line, the game is a draw.

```lisp
(game "Statuesque Sponge Of Judgment – v1"
    (players 2)

    ;; 5 × 5 square board
    (equipment
        (board (square 5))
    )

    (rules
        ;; Players take turns forever (or until an end rule fires)
        (play
            (repeat (P1 P2)
                (place (destination empty))
            )
        )

        ;; End conditions
        (end
            (if (line 4) (mover win))          ; four‑in‑a‑row → win
            (if (full_board) (draw))           ; board full → draw
        )
    )
)
```

*What we expressed*:  
* board definition, player count, a single “place” mechanic, and two simple end‑rules.  
All of this is covered by the most basic parts of the grammar (`board`, `play → once_through / repeat`, `place → destination`, `end → if …`).

---

## 2️⃣  Adding **capture of adjacent opponent stones**  

*New rule*: After a stone is placed, **all orthogonal + diagonal neighbours that belong to the opponent are captured** (removed) and the mover gains **one point per captured stone**.  
We keep the line‑of‑4 win condition.

```lisp
(game "Statuesque Sponge Of Judgment – v2"
    (players 2)

    (equipment
        (board (square 5))
    )

    (rules
        (play
            (repeat (P1 P2)
                (place
                    (destination empty)

                    ;; Capture every adjacent opponent piece and add a point for each.
                    (effects
                        (capture (adjacent any) increment_score:true)
                    )
                )
            )
        )

        (end
            (if (line 4) (mover win))
            (if (full_board) (draw))
        )
    )
)
```

*What changed & why it is a bit harder*:  

* The **`effects`** clause is now used inside a `place` mechanic.  
* `capture` needs a **mask** – we use the generic `adjacent any` mask, which matches all eight neighbours.  
* The optional `increment_score:true` argument tells the engine to increase the mover’s score by the number of captured stones.  
All of these constructs (`effects`, `capture`, `increment_score`) are part of the grammar but require a deeper nesting level.

---

## 3️⃣  Introducing a **score‑based win condition**  

*New rule*: The first player to reach **5 points** (from captures) also wins, regardless of whether a line of four has been made.

```lisp
(game "Statuesque Sponge Of Judgment – v3"
    (players 2)

    (equipment
        (board (square 5))
    )

    (rules
        (play
            (repeat (P1 P2)
                (place
                    (destination empty)
                    (effects
                        (capture (adjacent any) increment_score:true)
                    )
                )
            )
        )

        (end
            (if (line 4) (mover win))                         ; line of four → win
            (if (>= (score mover) 5) (mover win))             ; 5 points → win
            (if (full_board) (draw))
        )
    )
)
```

*What we added*:  

* The **function** `score` (wrapped in `>=`) lets us compare a player’s current score with a constant.  
* The new end‑rule `(if (>= (score mover) 5) (mover win))` shows how to use arithmetic predicates (`>=`) together with the `score` function – a step up in expressive power compared with the previous versions.

---

## 4️⃣  Full‑featured version – **forced passes** and **win‑by‑score**  

*New rules*:  

1. A player may **pass** if he has no legal move (i.e., no empty squares).  
2. If **both** players pass consecutively, the game ends and the player with the higher score wins (`by_score`).  
3. The previous win‑by‑line and win‑by‑score conditions stay in place.

```lisp
(game "Statuesque Sponge Of Judgment – v4"
    (players 2)

    (equipment
        (board (square 5))
    )

    (rules
        (play
            (repeat (P1 P2)
                (place
                    (destination empty)

                    (effects
                        (capture (adjacent any) increment_score:true)
                    )
                )
                ;; If a player cannot place (no empty squares), he may pass.
                (force_pass)
            )
        )

        (end
            (if (line 4) (mover win))                         ; line of four → win
            (if (>= (score mover) 5) (mover win))             ; 5 points → win
            (if (passed both) (by_score))                     ; both passed → higher score wins
            (if (full_board) (draw))                          ; board full with no winner → draw
        )
    )
)
```

*What makes this the hardest version*:  

* **`force_pass`** is a *stand‑alone* mechanic that can appear after a `place`. It tells the engine that the current player may voluntarily skip his turn when no legal destination exists.  
* The end‑rule `(if (passed both) (by_score))` uses the predicate `passed` with the special argument `both`, which is only available in the *end* section.  
* Combining **multiple win conditions** (`line`, `score`, `by_score`) demonstrates the full expressive power of the grammar’s predicate logic.

---

### Recap of the incremental steps  

| Step | New ludax concepts introduced |
|------|------------------------------|
| 1 – basic line win | `board`, `repeat`, simple `place`, `line`, `full_board` |
| 2 – capture effect | `effects`, `capture`, `adjacent`, `increment_score` |
| 3 – score‑based win | `function` `score`, arithmetic predicate `>=` |
| 4 – forced pass & by‑score | `force_pass`, predicate `passed`, end‑rule `by_score` |

Each snippet can be copied into a `.ludax` file and fed to a ludax‑compatible interpreter/compiler. Feel free to experiment – change the board size, the line length, the capture mask, or the winning score – and watch how the grammar cleanly accommodates the new rules. Enjoy playing *Statuesque Sponge Of Judgment*!

Below are three incremental implementations of **Wakeful Swinging Okapi** written in **ludax**.  
Each step adds a new rule that is a little harder to express with the language, while every snippet is a *stand‑alone, compilable* game description.

---

## 1️⃣  Simple version – “place‑only”

*   7 × 7 square board.  
*   Players alternate placing a stone on any empty square.  
*   First player to make a line of **5** stones (horizontal, vertical or diagonal) wins.  
*   If the board fills up with no line, the game is a draw.

```lisp
(game "Wakeful Swinging Okapi – Simple"
    (players 2)

    (equipment
        (board (square 7))
    )

    (rules
        (play
            (repeat (P1 P2)                     ; players take turns forever
                (place
                    (destination empty)        ; put a stone on any empty cell
                )
            )
        )
        (end
            (if (line 5) (mover win))           ; 5‑in‑a‑row → win
            (if (full_board) (draw))            ; board full → draw
        )
    )
)
```

---

## 2️⃣  Adding the **Swing‑Flip** mechanic  

After a stone is placed the player *must* flip every orthogonal‑adjacent opponent stone (the “swing”).  
A move is only legal if at least one adjacent opponent stone exists – otherwise the player must pass (handled later).

```lisp
(game "Wakeful Swinging Okapi – Swing Flip"
    (players 2)

    (equipment
        (board (square 7))
    )

    (rules
        (play
            (repeat (P1 P2)
                (place
                    (destination empty)

                    ; a move is only allowed when there is at least one adjacent opponent stone
                    (result
                        (exists
                            (adjacent (occupied opponent))
                        )
                    )

                    ; flip every adjacent opponent stone (the “swing”)
                    (effects
                        (flip (adjacent (occupied opponent)))
                    )
                )
            )
        )
        (end
            (if (line 5) (mover win))
            (if (full_board) (draw))
        )
    )
)
```

*`(adjacent (occupied opponent))`* is a **mask** that selects the four orthogonal neighbours that are occupied by the opponent.  
The `result` clause guarantees the move is only possible when the mask is non‑empty, and the `effects` clause flips exactly those stones.

---

## 3️⃣  Full game – scoring, forced passes & rendering  

Now we make the game richer:

*   Every flipped stone **increments** the player’s score.  
*   After each turn a player may **pass** if they have no legal placement (the `force_pass` rule).  
*   The game ends when a player reaches **10 points** *or* when both players have passed – the higher score wins.  
*   Simple colour rendering (black for P1, white for P2).

```lisp
(game "Wakeful Swinging Okapi – Full"
    (players 2)

    (equipment
        (board (square 7))
    )

    (rendering
        (color P1 black)
        (color P2 white)
    )

    (rules
        ; ---------- PLAY ----------
        (play
            (repeat (P1 P2)
                (place
                    (destination empty)

                    ; a move is legal only if at least one adjacent opponent stone exists
                    (result
                        (exists
                            (adjacent (occupied opponent))
                        )
                    )

                    ; flip all adjacent opponent stones
                    (effects
                        (flip (adjacent (occupied opponent)))

                        ; each flipped stone adds 1 point to the mover’s score
                        (increment_score mover
                            (count (adjacent (occupied opponent)))
                        )
                    )
                )
                ; if the current player cannot satisfy the “result” predicate,
                ; they may invoke a forced pass.
                (force_pass)
            )
        )

        ; ---------- END ----------
        (end
            ; a line of five still wins instantly
            (if (line 5) (mover win))

            ; reaching 10 points also wins
            (if (>= (score mover) 10) (mover win))

            ; both players passed → decide by score
            (if (passed both) (by_score))

            ; board full without a line → draw
            (if (full_board) (draw))
        )
    )
)
```

### What changed compared to version 2?

| Feature | ludax construct | Explanation |
|---------|----------------|-------------|
| **Score tracking** | `effect_increment_score` | Adds the number of flipped stones (`count (adjacent (occupied opponent))`) to the mover’s score each turn. |
| **Pass rule** | `(force_pass)` placed after the repeat block | Allows a player to skip their turn when the `result` predicate cannot be satisfied. |
| **End‑by‑score** | `if (>= (score mover) 10) (mover win)` | Declares a win as soon as a player reaches 10 points. |
| **Tie‑break by score** | `if (passed both) (by_score)` | When both players have passed, the player with the higher score wins; a draw is declared automatically if scores are equal. |
| **Rendering** | `(rendering …)` | Assigns black/white colours to the two players for a visual board. |

---

You now have a **complete, compilable** ludax description of *Wakeful Swinging Okapi* that starts simple and grows step‑by‑step, illustrating how increasingly sophisticated game mechanics can be expressed in the language. Enjoy playing (or extending) the game!

Below are **five** incremental implementations of the game **“Silky Smooth Dog”**.  
Each step adds a new rule that is *harder to express* in the Ludax language, while every version remains a **stand‑alone, compilable** Ludax program.

---

## 1️⃣  Simple “Silky Smooth Dog” – capture‑adjacent pieces only  

*Goal*: Place a piece on any empty square that is **adjacent to an opponent’s piece**.  
When you do, the adjacent opponent piece is captured (flipped) and you earn **one point**.  
First player to reach **5 points** wins; a full board without a winner is a draw.

```lisp
(game "Silky Smooth Dog – Simple Capture"
    (players 2)

    (equipment
        (board (square 5))               ; 5 × 5 board
    )

    (rules
        (play
            (repeat (P1 P2)               ; alternating turns
                (place
                    (destination empty)                     ; must go on an empty square
                    (result (exists (adjacent occupied opponent))) ; at least one opponent adjacent
                    (effects
                        (capture (adjacent occupied opponent) increment_score:true) ; flip & score
                    )
                )
                (force_pass)               ; if you cannot capture you may pass
            )
        )

        (end
            (if (>= (score mover) 5) (mover win))   ; 5 points → win
            (if (full_board) (draw))                ; board full → draw
        )
    )
)
```

---

## 2️⃣  Adding a **forced‑pass** rule  

Now a player **must pass** when **no legal capture move exists**.  
We express this by adding a *second* play‑mechanic that is only a `(force_pass)` and is guarded by a predicate that no capture is possible.

```lisp
(game "Silky Smooth Dog – Forced Pass"
    (players 2)

    (equipment
        (board (square 5))
    )

    (rules
        (play
            (repeat (P1 P2)
                ;--- Normal move -------------------------------------------------
                (place
                    (destination empty)
                    (result (exists (adjacent occupied opponent)))   ; must capture
                    (effects
                        (capture (adjacent occupied opponent) increment_score:true)
                    )
                )
                ;--- Pass move ---------------------------------------------------
                (place
                    (destination empty)               ; dummy – never used
                    (result (not (exists (adjacent occupied opponent)))) ; no capture possible
                )
                (force_pass)                           ; therefore you may pass
            )
        )

        (end
            (if (>= (score mover) 5) (mover win))
            (if (full_board) (draw))
        )
    )
)
```

*Explanation*:  
The second `(place …)` has a `result` that is true **only when** the player has **no adjacent opponent**.  
Because the destination is still required syntactically, we give a harmless `empty` mask; the move will never be chosen – the player will simply invoke the `(force_pass)` that follows it.

---

## 3️⃣  Bonus for forming a **line of three**  

If a placement creates a line of **exactly three** of the mover’s pieces, the mover gets an **extra point** (in addition to any capture points).

```lisp
(game "Silky Smooth Dog – Line Bonus"
    (players 2)

    (equipment
        (board (square 5))
    )

    (rules
        (play
            (repeat (P1 P2)
                (place
                    (destination empty)
                    (result (or
                               (exists (adjacent occupied opponent))   ; capture move
                               (line 3 exact:true)                     ; or line‑3 bonus
                           ))
                    (effects
                        (capture (adjacent occupied opponent) increment_score:true)
                        (increment_score mover (line 3))   ; +1 when a line‑3 is formed
                    )
                )
                (place
                    (destination empty)
                    (result (not (or
                                   (exists (adjacent occupied opponent))
                                   (line 3 exact:true)
                               )))
                )
                (force_pass)
            )
        )

        (end
            (if (>= (score mover) 5) (mover win))
            (if (full_board) (draw))
        )
    )
)
```

*Key points*  

* The `result` predicate now allows a move **either** because it captures **or** because it creates a line of three.  
* The `increment_score` effect adds the value returned by `(line 3)` (which is `1` when a line of three exists, otherwise `0`).  

---

## 4️⃣  Introducing **custodial captures** (sandwich rule)  

When a player places a piece that **sandwiches** exactly **two** opponent pieces between two of his own (a *custodial* pattern of length 2), **all sandwiched pieces are captured**.  
We keep the previous capture‑adjacent rule and the line‑bonus.

```lisp
(game "Silky Smooth Dog – Custodial Capture"
    (players 2)

    (equipment
        (board (square 5))
    )

    (rules
        (play
            (repeat (P1 P2)
                (place
                    (destination empty)
                    (result (or
                               (exists (adjacent occupied opponent))   ; normal capture
                               (line 3 exact:true)                     ; line bonus
                               (exists (custodial 2))                  ; custodial capture possible
                           ))
                    (effects
                        ;--- Adjacent capture -------------------------------------------------
                        (capture (adjacent occupied opponent) increment_score:true)

                        ;--- Custodial capture (sandwich of length 2) -------------------------
                        (capture (custodial 2) increment_score:true)

                        ;--- Line‑3 bonus ------------------------------------------------------
                        (increment_score mover (line 3))
                    )
                )
                (place
                    (destination empty)
                    (result (not (or
                                   (exists (adjacent occupied opponent))
                                   (line 3 exact:true)
                                   (exists (custodial 2))
                               )))
                )
                (force_pass)
            )
        )

        (end
            (if (>= (score mover) 5) (mover win))
            (if (full_board) (draw))
        )
    )
)
```

*Notes*  

* `custodial 2` means “a straight line of exactly two opponent pieces bounded on both sides by the mover’s pieces”.  
* The `capture` effect with that mask flips **all** such sandwiched pieces and also awards a point (`increment_score:true`).  

---

## 5️⃣  Full featured **Silky Smooth Dog** – with colors and score‑display  

The final version adds **rendering** (player colors) and **automatic score‑keeping** after every move, so the score is always up‑to‑date even when a player only passes.

```lisp
(game "Silky Smooth Dog – Full Version"
    (players 2)

    (equipment
        (board (square 5))
    )

    (rules
        (play
            (repeat (P1 P2)
                ;--- Normal move (capture, custodial, line‑3) -------------------------
                (place
                    (destination empty)
                    (result (or
                               (exists (adjacent occupied opponent))
                               (line 3 exact:true)
                               (exists (custodial 2))
                           ))
                    (effects
                        (capture (adjacent occupied opponent) increment_score:true)
                        (capture (custodial 2) increment_score:true)
                        (increment_score mover (line 3))

                        ;--- Keep scores in sync -----------------------------------------
                        (set_score mover (count (occupied mover)))
                        (set_score opponent (count (occupied opponent)))
                    )
                )
                ;--- Pass move (only allowed when no capture possible) ---------------
                (place
                    (destination empty)
                    (result (not (or
                                   (exists (adjacent occupied opponent))
                                   (line 3 exact:true)
                                   (exists (custodial 2))
                               )))
                )
                (force_pass)
            )
        )

        (end
            (if (>= (score mover) 5) (mover win))
            (if (full_board) (by_score))          ; winner by higher score
        )
    )

    (rendering
        (color P1 black)      ; P1’s pieces are black
        (color P2 white)      ; P2’s pieces are white
    )
)
```

### What this final version does

| Aspect | Rule |
|--------|------|
| **Board** | 5 × 5 square. |
| **Turn order** | Alternating (`repeat (P1 P2)`). |
| **Legal move** | Must place on an empty square **and** either capture an adjacent opponent piece, create a line of three, **or** perform a custodial (sandwich) capture of length 2. |
| **Capture** | Adjacent opponent pieces are flipped; custodial sandwiches of length 2 are also flipped. |
| **Scoring** | Each captured piece gives +1 point (`increment_score:true`). Forming a line of three gives an extra +1 point (`increment_score mover (line 3)`). After every move the scores are refreshed with `set_score`. |
| **Pass** | If none of the three capture conditions is possible, the player may pass (`force_pass`). |
| **Winning** | First to reach **5 points** wins; otherwise the game ends in a draw when the board fills, and the player with the higher score wins (`by_score`). |
| **Rendering** | P1 pieces are black, P2 pieces are white. |

---

### How to use these snippets

1. **Copy** any of the code blocks into a file with a `.ludax` extension.  
2. **Compile** with the Ludax compiler/interpreter you have.  
3. **Play** the game – the rules evolve as you move from the first snippet to the last, illustrating how increasingly complex mechanics can be expressed in Ludax.  

Enjoy playing **Silky Smooth Dog**!
